07 Strategic Anchors - YouTube.mp4
Okay, so we have our three-year target. We have our company purpose and we have our core values. Now we come to the final section of the Clarity Compass, and that's strategic anchors. If you recall, the purpose of... of the three-year target was to give us direction, a goal to pursue. To the south of the three-year target, we have our company purpose, and its job is to provide the momentum. fuel that drives us toward that three-year target. Then we have our core values and strategic anchors, which are designed to keep us on track, literally on the straight and narrow. But where core values address our behaviors, strategic anchors address our activities, and specifically— how we decide which activities to pursue.
In other words, strategic anchors give us a filter and framework for choosing which projects to say yes to and which— projects to say no to. And having a framework for saying no is really, really important as your company scales. Michael Porter, one of the foremost experts on business strategy, understood this when he said, 'The essence of strategy is choosing what not to do.' Similarly, David Packard, one of the co-founders and namesakes of Hewlett Packard, is quoted as saying, 'More organizations die of indigestion than starvation.' Did you catch that? More organizations die of indigestion than starvation. In other words, organizations die not because they don't do enough. They die because they try to do too much. So what's the lesson?
The lesson is, as the leader of a growing organization, you need a framework, a mechanism for saying no to projects and opportunities you should avoid. So you can leave room for the projects and opportunities where you have a clear competitive advantage. And that framework for saying yes and no is called a strategic anchor. So what are strategic anchors? Simply put, strategic anchors are what a company truly does best. It's competitive advantages. While strategic anchors... can be learned and added over time, they're typically a native genius, meaning their skills and expertise inherent to members of the founding team. Brainstorming your strategic anchors is a fairly simple process. First, create an exhaustive list of all your company's actions, activities, and even assets. Be as detailed as you can.
Seriously, list everything you do across all phases of the organization. And just as a side note, your value engines are a great source of ideas because they're quite literally a list of all the truly important things your company does. If you get stuck, open your value engines and I promise the ideas will really start flowing. Also, I like to use sticky notes and do this activity as a group. The group maximizes the flow. Ideas and the sticky notes make it easy to stack duplicate ideas and just move things around. But again, the goal at this stage is just to brain dump. So don't eliminate an action, activity, or asset because it seems too granular. Just stack it on a related sticky note and call it done.
Second, identify and sort the actions and activities by themes. Some anchors will relate to product creation, others sales and marketing, and still others general admin, or even something more narrow like licensing or strategic relationships. The point is, just put them in groups so we can begin distilling the brain dump into something more usable. Third, I want you to look at your list and ask the following: what combination of assets and capabilities do we possess that inform a truly defensible competitive advantage, just like crafting our list of core values? The goal should be to eliminate any strategic anchors that are permission to play. Said another way, if your competition can claim that they're just as good or worse, better than you are at something that's on your strategic anchor list, then it is not a strategic anchor.
As a side note, you can choose to develop an anchor if you believe it's an advantage worth owning, but don't add it to your list until you can say with confidence that you hold a truly defensible advantage over the competition. Finally, select three to five themes that summarize your competitive advantages and add them to your strategic anchor section on your clarity compass. Moving forward, anytime a new... project or opportunity presents itself, you can simply ask the following question. Are we playing to a known strength? If someone can't point to at least one and ideally more than one of your strategic anchors, then the decision should be to pass on the opportunity. Now, I won't lie— saying no is a painful, painful thing to do.
But it's not nearly as painful as dealing with the long-term consequences of saying yes to a project that you never should have started in the first place. And while I mentioned this before, it's worth repeating that strategic anchors can be added over time. Although it's not as common, new strategic anchors can simply be learned. More common, however, is that... Strategic anchors are acquired either by making the right hires or by acquiring the right company. In the mergers and acquisitions world, this is what's known as an acquisition. Higher. And it's one of the fastest ways to expand your company's competitive advantages. But for now, don't focus on expanding the number of your strategic anchors. Instead, focus on doubling down on the strategic anchors you already have. Because I promise, once you know what they are, you're going to identify opportunities you might have passed on that you now look at with green colored spectacles.
